artifacts.content:
Dear Emily and Michael,

I wanted to write this all down in one place before things get busy with the January 20th surgery at Providence Portland Medical Center on NE Glisan. Please read when you have a quiet moment.

First, I’m at peace with going ahead. The knee won’t get better on its own, and after those two falls — the curb in September and then the kitchen on 11/02 — I can’t keep living afraid of walking from the bedroom to the bathroom. You both know how shaken I was after the kitchen fall, needing the neighbor and EMS to get me up off the floor.

A couple of things I want you to remember:

1) My advance directives are already in place. My DNR and POLST form are on file at Providence Portland, signed back in early 2023. They say no CPR or intubation if my heart stops during or after surgery. This isn’t me “giving up”; it’s just what I decided after your dad died, and I still feel it’s right for me. If, heaven forbid, something goes wrong in the OR, I don’t want any arguments at the bedside — just follow what’s written.

2) Pain medicines: Dr. Kim at the Providence Internal Medicine Clinic on SE Belmont keeps reminding me that because of my mild chronic kidney disease and that old gastritis flare, I can’t take much in the way of NSAIDs. His 11/05 note literally says, “Avoid regular NSAID use due to CKD and prior gastritis flare.” So they’ll have to be careful with what they give me after the surgery. If I seem stoic, it’s not because it doesn’t hurt; it’s because I’m trying to protect the kidneys and my stomach.

3) I also talked to a counselor at Providence Behavioral Health on the phone in November after that kitchen fall. They called it an “adjustment disorder with anxiety related to fall risk,” which sounds dramatic, but really just means I get near-panic thinking about falling again. I’m a little embarrassed about it, but I want you to know in case I seem jumpy in the hospital or at home.

Logistics: Emily, thank you again for planning to stay those first two weeks. Michael, I know the drive from Seattle in January isn’t simple, but it means a lot that you’re trying to come down that first weekend.

Love you both more than you know,
Mom

Hi Pat,

Sitting here with my tea and the heating pad on this wretched left knee, so I thought I’d catch you up.

The pre-op with Dr. Laura McMillan at the Providence Orthopedic & Spine Institute on NE Glisan went fine overall. She’s very matter-of-fact, which I appreciate. She did mention (again) that my BMI is in the obese range and wants me to try to lose 10–15 pounds before the January 20th total knee replacement to lower the surgical risk and take some strain off the new joint. Easier said than done when standing long enough to cook anything more complicated than soup sets the knee on fire. I’m trying smaller portions and more vegetables from the freezer aisle.

I told her I’ve basically stopped going to in-person church since late 2023, not just because of the pain but because I feel so embarrassed shuffling in with the cane or a walker in front of people who remember me as the energetic second-grade teacher. She was kind about it, but it still stung to say out loud. I’ve mostly been watching the Methodist service online and singing along off-key in my living room.

On top of that, I’m fretting about the money. If the recovery is slow and they say I need a stint in a skilled nursing facility after the hospital, I’m not sure my little nest egg will stretch. Social Security only goes so far, and I told Emily back in December that I might have to dip into the last of the joint savings account your brother-in-law and I kept in that money market at U.S. Bank on NE Sandy. We always called it the “true emergency” fund; I suppose this qualifies, but it still makes my stomach twist.

And then there’s Emily’s situation. She’s arranged to work from home so she can stay overnight with me for those first two weeks, which I’m grateful for, but she admitted her husband has been grumbling about “you always running to your mom’s” and the strain it puts on their marriage. That breaks my heart. The last thing I want is to be the cause of tension in their house. I keep telling her if it gets to be too much, we’ll figure out another plan, but you know how stubborn she is.

Anyway, enough heavy stuff. How are things in Eugene? Are the rhodies in your yard as scraggly as mine these days?

Love,
Martha

Hi Carol,

Thank you again for the banana bread last Thursday — I’ve been having thin slices with my morning tea and pretending it’s as healthy as oatmeal.

I had my big pre-op appointment with Dr. McMillan at the Providence Orthopedic & Spine Institute on NE Glisan on 12/12. She went over everything for the left total knee arthroplasty on January 20 at Providence Portland Medical Center. Lots of talk about bone-on-bone and computer-assisted navigation that mostly went over my head, but I trust her. She did gently point out that my BMI is in the obese range and asked me to try to drop about 10 pounds before surgery. I nodded like a good patient, but between the knee pain and my love of your baked goods, we’ll see.

I also admitted to her that part of why I haven’t been at in-person services since late 2023 is pure embarrassment. I hate the idea of everyone at the Methodist church seeing me come in with the walker. I know that’s silly — half the congregation has some kind of mobility aid — but I still feel like they picture me as the lively teacher leading kids in songs, not the lady inching down the aisle. Watching online in my robe feels safer.

On the home front, Emily and I are making lists. We’re planning to set up a temporary sleeping spot on the main floor so I don’t have to do the basement stairs for laundry. We’re also borrowing a shower chair from one of her co-workers. The surgeon’s office mentioned a front-wheeled walker and a raised toilet seat, and maybe even a continuous passive motion machine if the physical therapist thinks I need it. I’m hoping insurance covers most of that; if not, I’ll grumble and then pay.

If you’re up for it, I’d love a quick visit the week before surgery — nothing fancy, just coffee at my kitchen table, and you can help me decide which plants I’m likely to kill while I’m hobbling around.

With love,
Martha

Hi Emily,

I called Providence Home Medical this morning about the walker and the raised toilet seat Dr. McMillan’s office mentioned. The good news: because it’s tied to the total knee replacement on 1/20, the front-wheeled walker should be mostly covered, and they’ll deliver it to the hospital. The toilet seat may have a small copay, but that’s manageable.

What’s keeping me up at night is the “what if” of needing a rehab stay afterward. Dr. Kim says lots of people my age go straight home with home health PT, especially with family around, but if something doesn’t go smoothly and they recommend a skilled nursing facility for a week or two, I’m worried about the copays. On just Social Security, there isn’t much wiggle room. Like I told you back in December, if they don’t cover the rehab stay, I’ll probably have to use what’s left in the U.S. Bank joint savings your dad and I kept in that money market on NE Sandy — the “true emergency” fund. I know that’s what we saved it for, but it still feels like crossing a line.

On the safety front, I finally did what you suggested after the November kitchen fall. I walked across the street and gave Karen Miller a spare house key. I told her, “If you don’t see my curtains open by 10 a.m., would you mind just checking on me?” She was very sweet about it, but saying it out loud made me feel about a hundred years old. Still, after needing EMS to haul me off the floor, I don’t want to be stubborn.

I’m going to try doing laundry only when you’re here on Sundays, so I’m not tackling the basement stairs alone. The memory of that step where my knee buckled is still too fresh.

Let’s talk this weekend about meals for the first couple of weeks home. Maybe we can stock the freezer with things that aren’t all salt and preservatives.

Love you,
Mom

To-do / thoughts before surgery – Martha – updated 1/12/25

Medical/logistics
- Surgery: Left total knee replacement with Dr. Laura J. McMillan at Providence Portland Medical Center, 4805 NE Glisan, on Monday 1/20/25. Check-in time still TBD.
- Remember: DNR + POLST already on file at Providence (signed early 2023). No CPR or intubation if heart stops. Tell Emily and Michael again so there’s no confusion if something happens.
- Pain meds: Because of mild chronic kidney disease and the old gastritis flare Dr. Kim documented at Providence Internal Medicine on SE Belmont, I have to avoid regular NSAIDs. His 11/05/24 note literally says, “Avoid regular NSAID use due to CKD and prior gastritis flare.” Make sure anesthesia and the floor nurses know I’m nervous about upsetting my kidneys and stomach.
- Mental health: Don’t forget to mention to the team that I spoke with the social worker at Providence Behavioral Health on the phone in November 2024 after the 11/02 kitchen fall. They called it “adjustment disorder with anxiety related to fall risk.” It sounds dramatic, but it’s real — I get almost panicky stepping into the tub or turning too fast.
- Weight: Dr. McMillan said at the 12/12 pre-op that my BMI is in the obese range and asked me to try to lose 10–15 lbs. Be kind to myself if it doesn’t all come off before 1/20.

Home/safety
- Emily staying overnight ~2 weeks after discharge. She’s juggling work and her husband’s grumbling about “you always running to your mom’s” — remember not to over-ask.
- Michael driving down from Seattle that first weekend if weather allows.
- Gave Karen Miller (across the street) a spare key after the November fall. Asked her to check if curtains aren’t open by 10 a.m. Don’t feel ashamed; it’s just smart.
- Laundry only with Emily here – no more solo trips to basement stairs.

Emotional
- Church: I’ve avoided in-person Methodist services since late 2023 because I’m embarrassed about the walker. Goal: go back in person by Easter, walker and all.
- Long-term goal: walk around Grant Park with the grandkids by late spring.

Event: Left Total Knee Arthroplasty – Inpatient Stay

Date: Monday, January 20, 2025
Time: 6:00 a.m. check-in (confirm when hospital calls)
Location: Providence Portland Medical Center, 4805 NE Glisan Street, Portland, OR 97213
Attendees: Martha; Emily (driving + staying first night)

Notes for myself:
- Surgeon: Dr. Laura J. McMillan, Providence Orthopedic & Spine Institute (Suite 2B). Procedure is elective left total knee arthroplasty with computer-assisted navigation.
- PCP: Dr. Daniel R. Kim at Providence Internal Medicine Clinic on SE Belmont. He’s the one who’s been warning about my kidneys and stomach — “Avoid regular NSAID use due to CKD and prior gastritis flare,” from his 11/05/24 note — so I need to remind anesthesia about mild chronic kidney disease and history of gastritis.
- I already have a DNR and POLST on file at Providence (signed early 2023) — no CPR or intubation if my heart stops during/after surgery. Emily and Michael are both aware; repeat it to them the night before so no one is surprised.
- Remember to mention to the nurses that I talked with a counselor at Providence Behavioral Health in November after the 11/02 kitchen fall and was told it’s “adjustment disorder with anxiety related to fall risk.” If I seem extra jumpy getting out of bed, that’s why.
- Dr. McMillan said at the 12/12 pre-op that my BMI is in the obese range and encouraged me to lose 10–15 pounds. I’ve managed maybe 5–6 so far — better than nothing.
- Equipment: Hospital should arrange delivery of front-wheeled walker and raised toilet seat. Ask about continuous passive motion (CPM) machine and whether it’s really necessary.
- Emily plans to stay overnight with me for the first few nights at home; Michael will try to visit the weekend after surgery if the passes aren’t icy.

Goal written here so I see it: By April 20, 2025 (three months out), walk around at least part of Grant Park with the grandkids without feeling like the knee will give way.

metadata.title: Surgery – Left Total Knee Replacement
metadata.location: Providence Portland Medical Center, 4805 NE Glisan Street, Portland, OR 97213

artifacts.content:
Dear Pastor Linda,

I hope the new year is treating you gently. I’ve been meaning to write instead of just lurking on the livestream every Sunday.

I’m scheduled for left total knee replacement surgery on Monday, January 20th at Providence Portland Medical Center on NE Glisan with Dr. Laura McMillan. My primary care doctor, Dr. Kim at the Providence Internal Medicine Clinic on SE Belmont, finally agreed that we’ve tried everything else. Because of my mild kidney issues and that old bout of gastritis, he keeps reminding me I can’t take many of the usual anti-inflammatory pain pills anymore, which has made this last year especially rough.

At my 12/12 pre-op visit, Dr. McMillan told me my BMI is in the obese range and gently suggested I try to lose 10–15 pounds before surgery to lower the risk and help the new joint. I’m doing what I can, but between the pain and my fondness for baked goods, it’s slow going.

I also need to confess something you may have already guessed: I haven’t been at in-person services since late 2023 not only because of the knee, but because I’ve felt embarrassed about showing up with a walker. It’s silly, I know — plenty of folks use canes and walkers — but I still picture myself as the energetic teacher leading kids in Christmas pageants, and I worry that’s how others remember me too. Watching the Methodist service online in my living room has felt safer, even if lonelier.

If you’re taking prayer requests, I would be grateful for prayers for a smooth surgery, manageable pain given my finicky kidneys and stomach, and courage to come back to the sanctuary in person once I’m steadier on my feet — walker and all. My quiet goal is to be able to join you in the pews by Easter.

Thank you for continuing to wave at the camera at the end of the livestream. It makes me feel a little less like a ghost.

With love,
Martha Henderson

metadata.subject: Prayer request before my knee operation

artifacts.content:
Subject: Surgery Preparations

Hi Emily,

I hope this email finds you well! I wanted to touch base about a few things before my surgery next week. Thank you so much for arranging to work from home and planning to stay with me. I can't tell you how much it means to have you nearby.

I've been keeping busy with the preparations and trying to stay positive. Dr. McMillan was very understanding during my last visit at Providence Orthopedic & Spine Institute. We talked about how hard it was to leave teaching when I did, even though it was the right decision for my health. Sometimes I regret retiring back in 2014, especially since I felt there was a bit of pressure from Mr. Johnson, the new principal. It seemed like he was subtly hinting that it might be time for me to step aside, which didn't sit well with me. But, then again, the knee pain was becoming a real issue.

Anyway, I'm trying to focus on the future and how wonderful it will be to walk without pain. I’m looking forward to the day when I can take the kids to Grant Park or maybe even bake some lemon bars again without having to sit down every few minutes.

I’ll have everything set up by the time you arrive, and I'm so relieved you'll be here to help. Let's chat soon to finalize the schedule and any last-minute details.

Love,
Mom

Dear Emily,

I hope this email finds you well. As you know, I've been preparing for my upcoming left total knee replacement surgery, scheduled for January 20, 2025, at Providence Portland Medical Center. I've been working closely with Dr. Daniel R. Kim and my orthopedic surgeon, Dr. Laura J. McMillan, to ensure everything is in place for a smooth procedure and recovery.

I've been experiencing more difficulty lately, as my left knee pain has become quite intense, and walking even short distances is challenging. Dr. Kim has noted that my condition has worsened, which is why they are expediting the surgery. The insurance company, Providence Medicare Advantage, should have all the necessary authorizations in place. My insurance ID is PVP123456789.

For the surgery, the planned procedure is an elective left total knee arthroplasty with computer-assisted navigation. Dr. McMillan and her team have outlined the necessary codes: M17.12 for the osteoarthritis, M25.562 for knee pain, and R26.2 for the walking difficulty. The procedure itself falls under CPT codes 27447, 73562, 97110, and 97161.

I've arranged for 12 physical therapy sessions after the surgery to aid my recovery, all within the Providence network to ensure everything is covered under my plan. The medical team is taking great care to avoid any out-of-network complications.

As for logistics, I'm relieved that you'll be able to work from home and stay with me for a bit after the surgery. Michael is planning to visit the weekend following the surgery, assuming all goes well with recovery and weather. It will be comforting to have you both around.

Please let me know if you need any more details or if there's anything else I should arrange in advance. I truly appreciate your support and will keep you updated as the surgery date approaches.

Love,
Mom
